cards.sentence: And for those people, I'm like, "You know what? You're just in the messy middle. Stick with it. This is par for the course."

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 이 상황에서 으레 겪게 되는, 지극히 정상적인 일
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase to normalize setbacks or difficulties, signaling to a team or colleague that what they are experiencing is a predictable and expected part of the process.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is reassuring founders who are feeling overwhelmed in the difficult middle stages of building a company. He explains that the chaos and uncertainty they feel is not a sign of failure — it is simply what this stage of the journey looks like for everyone.

vocabulary.meaning: the difficult, unglamorous stretch between a project's exciting start and its eventual success
vocabulary.note: A term Scott Belsky popularized in his book 'The Messy Middle' to describe the sustained grind of building.

cards.sentence: Well, the most humbling moments for me as a product leader have always been shoulder to shoulder to customers.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 나란히 붙어 함께 일하다; 현장에서 직접 맞대며 협력하다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase to emphasize hands-on collaboration or direct proximity with another group, particularly when contrasting it with a more distant or data-mediated relationship.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is reflecting on what has kept him grounded as a product leader throughout his career. He describes the experience of working directly with customers — not through reports or data, but in close, in-person contact — as consistently the most humbling and clarifying moments in his work.

vocabulary.meaning: causing a reduction in one's sense of importance or overconfidence, in a revealing and instructive way
vocabulary.note: Used here positively — the humility gained from direct customer contact is framed as a strength, not a weakness.

cards.sentence: I'm very long the experience economy because I think that there will be some people liberated to focus more on the non-scalable things that really move the needle for experiences for customers.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 눈에 띄는 변화를 만들어내다; 지표를 실질적으로 움직이다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase in business discussions when distinguishing between activities that produce real, measurable impact versus those that are busy work or marginal in effect.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is arguing that as AI handles more scalable, automated tasks, humans will become more valuable for delivering high-touch, non-scalable experiences. He believes investing in the experience economy is smart precisely because it is the human-driven, hard-to-replicate work that produces the most meaningful results for customers.

vocabulary.meaning: describing activities or approaches that cannot be easily expanded or automated to serve more people without proportionally more effort or cost
a business environment in which companies compete by delivering memorable, personalized experiences rather than just goods or services
vocabulary.note: A concept popularized by Pine and Gilmore (1999) and increasingly relevant in an AI-augmented world.

cards.sentence: I feel like this is where the best teams are going to build that muscle, that are going to go the distance.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 끝까지 버티며 완주하다; 긴 싸움에서 끝내 살아남다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when discussing long-term commitment or stamina in a project or competitive context, especially to praise or motivate a team facing a prolonged challenge.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is making the case that teams who develop the capacity to respond resourcefully to crises — rather than relying purely on abundant resources — are the ones who will endure and ultimately win. He frames this resilience as a competitive muscle that separates teams capable of long-term success from those that are not.

vocabulary.meaning: a capability or strength developed through repeated practice and deliberate effort
vocabulary.note: Used metaphorically here — 'build that muscle' means to deliberately develop a skill or organizational capacity over time.

cards.sentence: happen. But in terms of the half, the half-half, I learned this the hard way.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when sharing a hard-won personal insight to lend credibility and signal that the lesson comes from direct experience, not just theoretical knowledge.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is sharing a lesson about how to allocate a team's time between shipping new features and improving existing product quality. He acknowledges that the right balance — roughly splitting effort between the two — was not something he understood from the start, but rather a truth he had to discover through his own costly mistakes as a product leader.

cards.sentence: And so there's a lot of research around progress, be getting progress and how progress is a source of motivation. And so as product leaders, we have to merchandise progress.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 진척 상황을 팀과 이해관계자에게 적극적으로 가시화해 동기를 부여하다. merchandise = 상품을 진열·홍보하다에서 온 표현
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when discussing internal communications, team morale, or stakeholder updates — especially to argue for making intermediate milestones visible rather than only celebrating final launches.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is drawing on research showing that the perception of progress is itself a powerful motivator. He argues that product leaders must go beyond simply making progress — they have a responsibility to present and highlight that progress clearly to their teams, because people need to feel momentum to stay engaged during long development cycles.

vocabulary.meaning: to actively promote and make something visible in order to maximize its perceived value — here used as a verb to mean strategically showcasing progress
vocabulary.note: An unconventional use of the word: borrowed from retail (to 'merchandise' a product means to display it attractively), applied here to abstract internal communication.

cards.sentence: And so, we basically went on a killing spree. And we just started killing things.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 기능이나 프로젝트를 거침없이 대거 쳐내다; 망설임 없이 연달아 잘라버리다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this vivid phrase in informal or startup contexts to describe a rapid, decisive wave of cuts — whether eliminating product features, reducing a roadmap, or cutting low-value meetings and processes.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is describing a decisive period of ruthless product pruning at a company. Faced with an overgrown feature set that was dragging down the product, the team made an aggressive decision to rapidly cut a large number of things at once rather than incrementally trimming — treating the act of elimination as a bold, coordinated effort.

vocabulary.meaning: a period of unrestrained, rapid activity of a particular kind, usually done in quick succession
vocabulary.note: Originally used in contexts like 'shopping spree' or 'crime spree'; here repurposed darkly-humorously to describe aggressive product simplification.

cards.sentence: So my point number one is capitalize on the crisis, everyone. If resources are carbs, resourcefulness is like muscle.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 위기를 기회로 전환해 이득을 취하다; 어려운 상황을 역으로 이용해 도약하다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when making the case that a setback, funding cut, or market downturn can be reframed as a forcing function for innovation and efficiency, especially when addressing a leadership team or investors.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is sharing his first key piece of advice for teams navigating a period of scarcity or constraint. He argues that crises, rather than being purely negative, force teams to develop genuine resourcefulness — and that this resourcefulness, built under pressure, becomes a lasting competitive strength.

vocabulary.meaning: the ability to find creative, effective solutions with limited or constrained resources
vocabulary.note: Belsky contrasts it with 'resources' (external inputs like money) using an extended metaphor: resources are carbs (quick fuel) while resourcefulness is muscle (built strength).

cards.sentence: In fact, that's probably been the cheat code of my career as a product leader, has just been disproportionately empowering design throughout the process.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 남들이 모르는 결정적 비기(秘技); 적은 노력으로 압도적인 결과를 내는 숨겨진 공략법. 비디오게임의 치트 코드에서 온 표현
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase informally to describe an insight, habit, or practice that gives you an asymmetric edge — it signals both insider knowledge and a slightly playful tone, making it effective in pitch decks, conference talks, or team kick-offs.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is explaining what he considers his single most effective career lever as a product leader. He argues that giving design a disproportionately influential role — beyond what most organizations do — has been the strategic move that has consistently unlocked better product outcomes than any other approach he has used.

vocabulary.meaning: to a degree that is significantly greater than what is typical or expected relative to the whole
vocabulary.note: Key word here: Belsky is not just saying he values design, but that he invests in it at a level that is deliberately out of proportion to the norm.

cards.sentence: I think experienced product thinkers with that golden gut of, "Oh my gosh. Wait, reduction of cognitive load."

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 수년의 경험으로 단련된, 데이터 없이도 정확히 맞아떨어지는 황금 직관
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase to acknowledge or praise the earned intuition of a senior colleague, or to argue for the irreplaceable value of experienced judgment in product and design decisions — especially in contrast to purely data-driven approaches.

cards.context: Scott Belsky is arguing that experienced product thinkers possess a form of intuition that AI cannot easily replicate. He uses the phrase 'golden gut' to describe the deep, pattern-recognition-based instinct that allows a veteran product leader to instantly recognize — without needing data — when a design is adding too much cognitive load on a user.

vocabulary.meaning: the total amount of mental effort required to process information or complete a task
vocabulary.note: A core concept in UX design: reducing cognitive load means making a product easier to understand and use without overwhelming the user's attention.

speaker.name: Scott Belsky
speaker.title: Founder of Behance, former Adobe CPO, investor
speaker.titleHistory: Founder of Behance · Former Chief Product Officer at Adobe · Investor
speaker.motif: a glowing design grid with a spark igniting at its center, radiating outward into flowing brushstrokes
speaker.introLine: Design is not merely decoration—it is the bridge between vision and action, and those who master this craft shape markets.
speaker.sealLine: You have learned that true product leadership flows from empowering design as a strategic force, not an afterthought.
